title: Analytic study and statistical enforcement of extended beta functions imposed by Mittag-Leffler and Hurwitz-Lerch Zeta functions
author: Abdulnabi, Al-Janaby, et al.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Proposed Beta- Mittag-Leffler-Hurwitz-Lerch Zeta Function Bδ,ϱ,ητ,μ(ξ1,ξ2)

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: This section presents a new extended Beta function, indicated by Bδ,ϱ,ητ,μ(ξ1,ξ2), related to the Mittag-Leffler (9) and the HL-Z (12) functions using the convolution principle (1).

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: The Mittag-Leffler function (Wiman's function) is expressed in (9) as:Eγ,ϱ(z)=∑κ=0∞zκΓ(κγ+ϱ),(R(γ),R(ϱ)>0).

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: For γ=1, the function Eγ,ϱ(z) turns to(22)E1,ϱ(z)=∑κ=0∞zκΓ(κ+ϱ),(R(ϱ)>0).

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Furthermore, the HL-Z function HL*(z,τ,μ,η) is stated in (12) as:HL*(,τ,μ,η,z)=∑κ=0∞(τ)κκ!zκ(κ+η)μ,(μ,τ∈C, η∈C∖Z0−, when |z| 1, when |z|=1).

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Thus, from (1), (22), and (12), the following new function is acquired:(23)ψϱ,ητ,μ(z)=HL*(τ,μ,η,z)*Γ(ϱ)E1,ϱ(z)=∑κ=0∞(τ)κ(ϱ)κ(κ+η)μzκκ!.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Remark 1. For the applicable parameters, τ,μ and ϱ, the function ψϱ,ητ,μ(z) written in (23) reduces to specified functions. Hence, the following special cases are yielded:1.ψ1,η1,0(z)=ez,2.ψϱ,ητ,0(z)=1F1(τ;ϱ;z) given in (4).

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Based on Eq. (23), the new extended Beta function Bδ,ϱ,ητ,μ(ξ1,ξ2) is expressed by the following definition.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Definition 1. For δ≥0, the extended Beta function Bδ,ϱ,ητ,μ(ξ1,ξ2) is formulated as:(24)Bδ,ϱ,ητ,μ(ξ1,ξ2)=∫01℘ξ1−1(1−℘)ξ2−1ψϱ,ητ,μ(−δ(℘(1−℘))−1)d℘,where min{R(ξ1),R(ξ2),R(τ),R(μ),R(ϱ),R(η)}>0.

steps.title: The noteworthy special cases of extended Beta function Bδ,ϱ,ητ,μ(ξ1,ξ

description: In mathematics, complex analysis discusses the analytic functions of complex numbers. The term complex numbers acquire extensive utilization in various realms of mathematics, especially in functional analysis, see [[1], [2], [3], [4]]. The theory of special function (SFT) is a prominent and captivating theme in complex analysis. Special Functions (SFs) are created by solving numerous partial differential equations and physical problems, which are usually generalized formulas of elementary functions. At that point, SFs are mathematical functions that act in complex domains, have private merits, and are often named after the initials of scientists who innovated them. Notable examples include the hypergeometric function, Wright function, Mittag-Leffler function, Meijer G-function, Hurwitz zeta function, and others. Remarkably, diverse representations of SFs, such as power series (special series) or integral representations, are utilized in mathematical evaluation. This appeals in mathematical processes to create SFs, namely convolution (Hadamard) product and symbolized by *. It was first posed by Hadamard as [5]: for analytical power series ω1(z)=∑κ=0∞ρκ,1zκ and ω2(z)=∑κ=0∞ρκ,2zκ in consecutive disks Δ1 and Δ2 centered at the origin, the convolution product of ω1 and ω2, represented by θ1*θ2, produces a third power series expressed as:(1)(ω1*ω2)(z)=∑κ=0∞ρκ,1ρκ,2zκ. The vital idea of SFs lies in the effect of their complex parameters and variables in resolving and interpreting numerous scientific problems. Therefore, SFs are essential in developing ongoing investigations. Interestingly, Special Function Theory (SFT) is closely connected to Geometric Function Theory (GFT), which explores the geometric merits of analytical functions acting in a complex open unit disk. In this context, several investigators contributed greatly to its development, see ([6,7]). Indeed, the intensive study of SFs has occupied a major position in the scientific disciplines of applied mathemati
